elements.sent-2411-010: Rooted in the explosion of deep learning over the past decade, this thesis spans from AlphaGo to ChatGPT to empirically examine the fundamental concepts needed to realize the vision of an artificial scientist: a machine with the capacity to autonomously generate original research and contribute to the expansion of human knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-011: The investigation begins with {\sc Olivaw , an AlphaGo Zero-like agent that discovers Othello knowledge from scratch but is unable to communicate it.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This realization leads to the development of the Explanatory Learning (EL) framework, a formalization of the problem faced by a scientist when trying to explain a new phenomenon to their peers.
elements.sent-2411-013: The effective EL prescriptions allow us to crack Zendo, a board game simulating the scientific endeavor.
elements.sent-2411-014: This success comes with a fundamental insight: an artificial scientist must develop its own interpretation of the language used to explain its findings.
elements.sent-2411-015: This perspective then leads us to see modern multimodal models as interpreters, and to devise a new way to build interpretable and cost-effective CLIP-like models: by coupling two unimodal models using little multimodal data and no further training.
elements.sent-2411-016: Finally, we discuss what ChatGPT and its siblings are still missing to become artificial scientists, and introduce Odeen, a benchmark about interpreting explanations that sees LLMs going no further than random chance while being instead fully solved by humans.

elements.sent-2411-032: It has been 25 years since a report of original research was last submitted to our editors for publication, making this an appropriate time to revisit the question that was so widely debated then: what is the role of human scientists in an age when the frontiers of scientific inquiry have moved beyond the comprehensibility of humans? No doubt many of our subscribers remember reading papers whose authors were the first individuals ever to obtain the results they described.
elements.sent-2411-033: But as metahumans began to dominate experimental research, they increasingly made their findings available only via DNT (digital neural transfer), leaving journals to publish second-hand accounts translated into human language.
elements.sent-2411-034: Without DNT, humans could not fully grasp earlier developments nor effectively utilize the new tools needed to conduct research, while metahumans continued to improve DNT and rely on it even more.
elements.sent-2411-035: Journals for human audiences were reduced to vehicles of popularization, and poor ones at that, as even the most brilliant humans found themselves puzzled by translations of the latest findings.
elements.sent-2411-036: No one denies the many benefits of metahuman science, but one of its costs to human researchers was the realization that they would probably never make an original contribution to science again.
elements.sent-2411-037: Some left the field altogether, but those who stayed shifted their attentions away from original research and toward hermeneutics: interpreting the scientific work of metahumans.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the year 2000, on the pages of Nature, the esteemed science-fiction author Ted Chiang depicts a world that appears to offer no more room for human scientists.
elements.sent-2411-039: Superior entities has taken the lead in research, and communicate their findings only within themselves through an inaccessible medium \cite{chiang2000catching .}
elements.sent-2411-040: This scenario seems the one we will be condemned to with the advent of artificial scientists: in the future, machines alone will be responsible for making the discoveries necessary to refine our tools and therapies.
elements.sent-2411-041: They are faster, with greater memory, capable of making associations beyond our reach.
elements.sent-2411-042: Most importantly, they will not be hampered by the bottleneck of our language, free to surpass our reasoning limits.
elements.sent-2411-043: Are we condemned to not understand future breakthroughs, to give up on the once glorious practice of science as a species? \begin{flushright {\slshape Se non ci divertiamo a risolvere questo problema perché dovremmo studiarlo?\footnote{If solving this problem isn't fun for us, then what's the point of delving into it? --- Nicola Cabibbo (\href{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicola_Cabibbo {Wiki ) \end{flushright Not at all.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: And not because we will not have those formidable machines.
elements.sent-2411-045: This fear comes from a fundamental misrepresentation of what science truly is: a playful pursuit of our curiosity.
elements.sent-2411-046: That stands as a vital part of the human culture–-as Ted Chiang reminds us in the latter half of his story-–rooted in creativity, experience, and, ultimately, communication.
elements.sent-2411-047: Science is the art of explaining nature to humans, there is no science beyond our language.\footnote{This is less restrictive than it might seem, as our language is not a rigid cage but rather a dynamic entity that evolves to meet our communicative needs.}
elements.sent-2411-048: As we will discuss and experimentally test in the following.
elements.sent-2411-049: Nevertheless, machines can partake in this infinite quest alongside us.
elements.sent-2411-050: Indeed, this is the perspective we will adopt in the following pages: How can we devise a machine capable of making its own discoveries and then effectively explain them to us? \pagestyle{scrheadings \cleardoublepage \refstepcounter{dummy \pdfbookmark[1]{\contentsname {tableofcontents \setcounter{tocdepth {2 \setcounter{secnumdepth {3 \manualmark \markboth{\spacedlowsmallcaps{\contentsname {\spacedlowsmallcaps{\contentsname \automark[section]{chapter \renewcommand{\chaptermark [1]{\markboth{\spacedlowsmallcaps{#1 {\spacedlowsmallcaps{#1 \renewcommand{\sectionmark [1]{\markright{\thesection\enspace\spacedlowsmallcaps{#1 \begingroup \let\relax \let\cleardoublepage\relax \let\cleardoublepage\relax \refstepcounter{dummy \pdfbookmark[1]{\listfigurename {lof \listoffigures \refstepcounter{dummy \pdfbookmark[1]{\listtablename {lot \listoftables \endgroup \cleardoublepage\pagenumbering{arabic \cleardoublepage.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Explanations are the fuel of progress, the fundamental tool through which humans have increased their agency, earning more and more control over their future throughout history.

elements.sent-2411-060: This will lead us to realize the tension between language and the effective representation of new natural phenomena: language must be adapted to the new communicative need, but at the same time it should remain understandable; its vocabulary and rules slowly but inevitably change over time.

elements.sent-2411-062: As a result, we will introduce Explanatory Learning and place the keystone of this thesis: a true artificial scientist can only emerge when a machine can autonomously interpret symbols.

elements.sent-2411-076: We introduce {\scshape Olivaw , an AI Othello player adopting the design principles of the famous AlphaGo programs.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The main motivation behind {\scshape Olivaw was to discover knowledge from scratch in a non-trivial board game at a tiny fraction of the cost of its illustrious predecessors.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In this chapter, we show how the AlphaGo Zero's paradigm can be successfully applied to the popular game of Othello using only commodity hardware and free cloud services.
elements.sent-2411-079: While being simpler than Chess or Go, Othello maintains a considerable search space and difficulty in evaluating board positions.
elements.sent-2411-080: To achieve this result, {\scshape Olivaw implements some improvements inspired by recent works to accelerate the standard AlphaGo Zero learning process.}
elements.sent-2411-081: The main modification implies doubling the positions collected per game during the training phase, by including also positions not played but largely explored by the agent.
elements.sent-2411-082: We tested the strength of {\scshape Olivaw in three different ways: by pitting it against Edax, considered by many the strongest open-source Othello engine, by playing anonymous games on the web platform OthelloQuest, and finally in two in-person matches against top-notch human players: a national champion and a former world champion.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Only a year after AlphaGo's landmark victory against Go master Lee Sedol another sensational development took place.
elements.sent-2411-084: An improved version of AlphaGo called AlphaGo Zero asserted itself as the strongest Go player in the history of the game \citep{Silver2017MasteringKnowledge .}
elements.sent-2411-085: The remarkable feature of AlphaGo Zero was that, unlike its predecessor and unlike all previous game software, it learned to master the game entirely by itself, without any human knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-086: As subsequent follow-up work quickly showed, AlphaGo's paradigm-- an interesting blend of deep and reinforcement learning-- seems to be general and flexible enough to adapt to a wide array of games \citep{Silver2018ASelf-play., muzero-schrittwieser2019mastering .}
elements.sent-2411-087: These extraordinary successes came at a price however, and quite literally so.
elements.sent-2411-088: The amount of computational and financial resources that were required was so huge as to be out of reach for most academic and non-academic institutions.
elements.sent-2411-089: Not coincidentally these well-endowed projects and their follow-ups took place within giant multinational corporations of the IT sector \citep{tian2019elf,leeminigo .}
elements.sent-2411-090: These companies deployed GPUs by the thousands and hundreds of TPUs.
elements.sent-2411-091: A recent study looked at the number of petaflops per day that were required to train AlphaGo Zero and other recent well-known results in AI \citep{amodei2018ai .}
elements.sent-2411-092: The paper shows an exponential growth with a 3.4-month doubling period.
elements.sent-2411-093: This is clearly unsustainable for most academic labs and departments and even the greatest majority of companies.
elements.sent-2411-094: Another aspect of the same problem is the amount of training needed.
elements.sent-2411-095: AlphaGo Zero required 4.9 million games played during self-play.
elements.sent-2411-096: And in order to attain the level of grandmaster for games like Starcraft II and Dota 2 the training required 200 years and more than $10,000$ years of gameplay, respectively \citep{vinyals2019grandmaster, pachockiopenai .}
elements.sent-2411-097: Thus one of the major problems to emerge in the wake of these breakthroughs is whether comparable results can be attained at a much lower computational and financial cost and with just commodity hardware.
elements.sent-2411-098: In this chapter we take a small step in this direction, by showing that AlphaGo Zero's successful paradigm can be replicated for the game of Othello (also called Reversi).
elements.sent-2411-099: While being much simpler than either Chess or Go, this game is still rather sophisticated and has a considerable strategic depth.
elements.sent-2411-100: The game enjoys a long history and a rich tradition.
elements.sent-2411-101: Every year an exciting world championship takes place in which accomplished players from all over the world vie for the world title.
elements.sent-2411-102: Our Othello engine is called {\sc Olivaw , a homage to the famous robot character invented by Isaac Asimov.}
elements.sent-2411-103: We tested the strength of {\sc Olivaw in three different ways.}

elements.sent-2411-105: Perhaps the most interesting aspect of this set of matches was that {\sc Olivaw managed to beat several times an opponent that explores tens of millions of positions in the game tree in the course of a single game.}
elements.sent-2411-106: In contrast, {\sc Olivaw 's search of the game tree was limited to a couple of thousand positions.}
elements.sent-2411-107: We also tested {\sc Olivaw against (presumably) human players of varying strength on the web platform OthelloQuest.}
elements.sent-2411-108: But the most exciting challenge was a series of matches against top-notch human players: a national champion and a former world champion.
elements.sent-2411-109: The final outcome shows that in a relatively short training time {\sc Olivaw reached the level of the best human players in the world.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Crucially, this has been achieved by using very limited resources at very low overall cost: commodity hardware and free, and thus very limited, cloud services.

elements.sent-2411-133: Othello is a popular board game.
elements.sent-2411-134: Its simple rules are explained in Figure \ref{fig:rules .}
elements.sent-2411-135: A typical game lasts for some 60 moves, with an average branching factor of 10.
elements.sent-2411-136: Like Go and Chess it is a perfect information game.
elements.sent-2411-137: Although simpler than these two, it has considerable strategic depth.
elements.sent-2411-138: Unlike English draughts, there is no known perfect strategy that can be played by computer \cite{mullins2007checkers .}
elements.sent-2411-139: Othello is played across the globe.
elements.sent-2411-140: There are professional players competing in official tournaments organized by world and national federations.
elements.sent-2411-141: The Othello world championship takes place every year.
elements.sent-2411-142: The best software beat humans systematically but, as discussed, they rely on brute force for most of the game.
elements.sent-2411-143: During the initial stages of the game, they access huge databases of openings, which are known to be very important in Othello.
elements.sent-2411-144: An opening lasts between 10 and 20 moves.
elements.sent-2411-145: Furthermore, some 20-30 moves before the end they play the perfect game by exploring the game tree in its entirety.
elements.sent-2411-146: In the middle game too, these programs explore hundreds of millions of positions of the game tree per move, a clear indication of brute force at play.
elements.sent-2411-147: In contrast, as we shall see, {\sc Olivaw explores only a few hundreds positions and does not use any database of openings.}

elements.sent-2411-148: The main difference consists of a somewhat different and cheaper training process.
elements.sent-2411-149: The network architecture, while mimicking that of AlphaGo Zero, was scaled down.
elements.sent-2411-150: Before discussing {\sc Olivaw in detail, it is useful to describe its basic design.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Like the AlphaGo \rev{programs , {\sc Olivaw uses reinforcement learning to build an ``oracle'', in the form of a deep network $f_\theta$ ($\theta$ denotes the weights of the neural network).}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Given as input an Othello game state $s$, $f_\theta$ outputs a pair: $f_\theta(s) = (\bm{p , v)$.}
elements.sent-2411-153: The vector $\bm{p}$ is a probability distribution over the possible moves from $s$.
elements.sent-2411-154: Intuitively, the higher the probability the better the move.
elements.sent-2411-155: The value $v$ is the oracle's assessment of how good state $s$ is, ranging from $+1$ (sure victory) to $-1$ (certain defeat).
elements.sent-2411-156: The oracle is used to guide an exploration of the \rev{``possible near futures'' by a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) \cite{mcts-browne2012survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-157: To pick the next move, the game tree rooted at $s$ is explored.
elements.sent-2411-158: Roughly speaking, in this exploration, the moves that $f_\theta$ considers good are explored first (so that the \rev{actual branching factor is limited ) and the total number of nodes explored is limited (in the few hundreds during training and set to one thousand when playing against humans).}
elements.sent-2411-159: The goal of this exploration phase is to produce a better estimate $(\bm{\pi , q)$ of state $s$.}
elements.sent-2411-160: When this is done, the best move according to $\bm{\pi}$ is played to reach a new state $s'$, and the process is repeated.
elements.sent-2411-161: What is noteworthy about this process is that while by itself $f_\theta$ is a rather weak player, using it in combination with MCTS gives rise to a very strong one, i.e. the estimates $(\bm{\pi , q)$ are more reliable than $(\bm{p , v)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Let us call $A(f)$ the MCTS playing agent using $f$ as oracle.
elements.sent-2411-163: The crux of the approach is to generate a sequence of oracles $f_0,$ $ f_1, $ $f_2, $ $\ldots, $ $f_t$ each better than the predecessors.
elements.sent-2411-164: This is done by generating a sequence of training sets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_t$ each better than the previous one.
elements.sent-2411-165: Training set $S_i$ is used to train $f_i$.
elements.sent-2411-166: The process is initialized with a deep network $f_0$ with random weights.
elements.sent-2411-167: The generic step in this sequence of improvements is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-169: During the so-called self-play phase, $A(f_\theta)$ plays a batch of games against itself.
elements.sent-2411-170: During each game a set of states $S$ will be explored.
elements.sent-2411-171: For each $s \in S$ an updated (and hopefully better) assessment $(\bm{\pi _s, q_s)$ will be computed.}
elements.sent-2411-172: The set $T$ of pairs $\{s, (\bm{\pi _s, q_s)\}$ for $s \in S$ will be added to the training set.}
elements.sent-2411-173: The intuition is that this way we can create a virtuous circle.
elements.sent-2411-174: As the assessments $(\bm{\pi _s, q_s)$ become more and more accurate the training set becomes better and better.}
elements.sent-2411-175: And, as the training set improves the assessment becomes more accurate.
elements.sent-2411-176: We remark \rev{that the main difference between {\sc Olivaw and AlphaGo Zero resides in how this training set is constructed.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Instead of $\{s,$ $ (\bm{\pi _s, $ $ q_s)\}$, AlphaGo Zero only considers pairs $\{s, (\bm{\pi _s, z_s)\}$ where $s$ is actually played during the game, and $z_s$ is the outcome at the end of the game.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: In contrast, besides this type of pairs, {\sc Olivaw also adds to the training set pairs $\{s, (\bm{\pi _s, q_s)\}$ for which $s$ has been explored ``a lot''.}}
elements.sent-2411-180: In this way, we collect a larger training set for the same number of simulated games.
elements.sent-2411-181: This is crucial since the cost of the self-play phase is the main contributor to the overall cost.
elements.sent-2411-182: This design choice is discussed in detail in section \ref{sec-comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Once the new training set is obtained we switch to the training phase.
elements.sent-2411-184: The current neural network $f_\theta$ is further trained with the updated training set.
elements.sent-2411-185: At the end of this training we have a new configuration $f_\theta'$.
elements.sent-2411-186: We want $(\bm p, v) = f_\theta'(s)$ to be close to $($\boldmath$\pi\,$\unboldmath$, \omega)$, where $\omega$ can be $z \;\mathrm{or}\; q$.
elements.sent-2411-187: So we minimize the training loss: \begin{equation}L(\bm{\pi}, \omega, \bm{p}, v) = (\omega - v)^2 - \bm{\pi}^T\log{\bm{p}} + c||\theta||^2\end{equation} As in AlphaGo Zero, we combine evenly the squared error on the value and the cross-entropy on the move probabilities, while the last term penalizes large weights in the neural network ($c=10^{-4}$).
elements.sent-2411-188: This L2 regularization is used to prevent overfitting over the many training phases.
elements.sent-2411-189: In the final evaluation phase, we verify whether the new $f_\theta'$ is stronger than the old $f_\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-190: To do so, we pit $A(f_\theta')$ against $A(f_\theta)$.
elements.sent-2411-191: If the former wins significantly more often than the latter it becomes the new oracle.
elements.sent-2411-192: Otherwise we go through the training phase again to produce a new challenger.
elements.sent-2411-193: The whole process is then repeated.

elements.sent-2411-258: When selecting a move from state $s$ with MCTS, in the root node we add Dirichlet noise to the prior probabilities computed by the neural network oracle: \begin{equation}P(s_0, a) = (1 - \epsilon)p_a + \epsilon x_a \;\;\;\; \bm{x} \in \mathbb{R}^B\end{equation} Where $B$ is the number of legal moves from $s_0$, and $\bm{x}$ is a point sampled from the symmetric Dirichlet probability distribution $X_\alpha$, with $\alpha=\mathrm{min (1, 10 / B)$, we used an $\epsilon = 0.25$ as in AlphaGo Zero.}
elements.sent-2411-259: A symmetric Dirichlet noise with an $\alpha < 1$ tends to unbalance the move probability distribution $\pi(a|s_0)$ towards a specific action, favoring an exploration in depth of the subsequent variant.
elements.sent-2411-260: In games with high branching factor, this behavior is desired to preserve the asymmetric nature of the MCTS search.
elements.sent-2411-261: So, the higher the branching factor of the game, the lower the $\alpha$ parameter of the symmetric Dirichlet noise used ($\alpha = 0.03$ in AlphaGo Zero).

elements.sent-2411-263: If during a game a player values a position under a resignation threshold $v_{\mathrm{resign }$, the game ends and the opponent is considered the winner.}
elements.sent-2411-264: In early generations {\sc Olivaw plays $10\%$ of the games until the end, \rev{i.e. the first 250 games.}}

elements.sent-2411-269: Concerning the architecture, {\sc Olivaw uses a Residual Network \cite{He2015DeepRecognition as AlphaGo Zero.}}
elements.sent-2411-270: The game state input $s$ is a $8 \times 8 \times 2$ binary tensor in {\sc Olivaw , in contrast to the deeper $19\times 19 \times 17$ binary tensor of AlphaGo Zero.}
elements.sent-2411-271: We do not need layers representing past positions in Othello since the game state is fully observable from the board position.
elements.sent-2411-272: Even the information on the player's turn is not necessary since Othello is symmetrical with respect to the player, we can assume that the turn player is always white, flipping all the discs if it is the turn of black.

elements.chapter-2411-009: Explanatory Learning: Beyond Empiricism in Neural Networks
elements.sent-2411-340: At the crossroads of Program Synthesis and Meta-Learning, we introduce Explanatory Learning as the task of automatically discovering the symbolic explanation (PS) that enables few-shot sensible predictions on a novel environment given experience on other environments (M-L).
elements.sent-2411-341: Differently from PS, the program (explanation) interpreter in EL is not given and should be learned from a limited collection of associations explanation-observations.
elements.sent-2411-342: Unlike M-L, EL does not prescribe any adaptation at test time, seeking generalization in the broad meanings attributed to symbols by the learned interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-343: To exemplify the challenges of EL, we present the Odeen benchmark, which can also serve the PS and M-L paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-344: Finally, we introduce Critical Rationalist Networks, a deep learning approach to EL aligned with the rationalist view of knowledge acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-345: CRNs express several desired properties by construction; they are truly explainable, can adjust their processing at test-time for harder inferences, and can offer strong confidence guarantees on their predictions.
elements.sent-2411-346: Using Odeen as a testbed, we show how CRNs outperform empiricist end-to-end approaches of similar size and architecture (Transformers) in discovering explanations for unseen environments.
elements.sent-2411-347: Making accurate predictions about the future is a key ability to survive and thrive in a habitat.
elements.sent-2411-348: Living beings have evolved many systems to this end, such as memory \cite{mcconnell1962memory , and several can predict the course of complex phenomena \cite{Taylor16389 .}}
elements.sent-2411-349: However, no animal comes even close to the prediction ability of humans, which stems from a unique-in-nature system.
elements.sent-2411-350: At the core of this system lies an object called explanation, formed by the proposition of a language, which has a remarkable property: it can be installed with ease into another human speaking the same language, allowing to make predictions on new phenomena without ever having experienced them.
elements.sent-2411-351: When the installation is successful, we say that the human has understood the explanation.

A convenient setting to study and test the process of knowledge discovery in machines. Like the night sky was for humans. $\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\,${ $^1$Galileo did not sketch negative examples.}} \label{fig-galileo}
elements.sent-2411-352: This process is key to the success of human beings.
elements.sent-2411-353: An individual can provide accurate predictions for a multitude of phenomena without going through a painful discovery process for all of them, but only needs an operating system -- mastering a language -- and someone who communicates the relevant explanations; this way, the individual can focus on unexplained phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-354: When an explanation is found for them, it is added to the existing shared collection, which we call knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-355: How can we make machines take part in this orchestra? With this work, we try to shed new light on this problem.
elements.sent-2411-356: Specifically, we propose a learning procedure to allow machines (i) to {\em understand existing explanations, in the sense described above, and (ii) create new explanations for unexplained phenomena, much like human scientists do.}
elements.sent-2411-357: First sketch on the left is compatible with the rule since the fourth moon can be hidden by one of the other moons or by Jupyter itself.
elements.sent-2411-358: Our contribution in this sense is threefold:.
elements.sent-2411-359: We formulate the challenge of creating a machine that masters a language as the problem of learning an interpreter from a collection of examples in the form explanation-observations.
elements.sent-2411-360: The only assumption we make is this dual structure of data; explanations are free strings, and are not required to fit any formal grammar.
elements.sent-2411-361: This results in the Explanatory Learning (EL) framework described in Sec.~\ref{sec-el .}
elements.sent-2411-362: We present Odeen, a basic environment to test EL approaches, which draws inspiration from the board game Zendo \cite{zendo .}
elements.sent-2411-363: Odeen simulates the work of a scientist in a small universe of simple geometric figures, see Figure \ref{fig-galileo .}
elements.sent-2411-364: We present it in Sec.~\ref{sec-odeen , and will release it upon publication.}
elements.sent-2411-365: We argue that the dominating empiricist ML approaches are not suitable for EL problems.
elements.sent-2411-366: We propose Critical Rationalist Networks (CRNs), a family of models designed according to the epistemological philosophy pushed forward by \citet{popper1935logic .}
elements.sent-2411-367: Although a CRN is implemented using two neural networks, the working hypothesis of such a model does not coincide with the adjustable network parameters, but rather with a language proposition that can only be accepted or refused in toto.
elements.sent-2411-368: We will present CRNs in Sec.~\ref{sec-crns , and test their performance on Odeen in Sec.~\ref{sec-exp .}}
elements.sent-2411-369: Humans do not master a language from birth.
elements.sent-2411-370: A baby can not use the message ``this soap stings'' to predict the burning sensation caused by contact with the substance.
elements.sent-2411-371: Instead, the baby gradually learns to interpret such messages and make predictions for an entire universe of phenomena \cite{schulz2007preschool .}
elements.sent-2411-372: We refer to this state of affairs as mastering a language, and we aim to replicate it in a machine as the result of an analogous learning process.
elements.sent-2411-373: Using a batch of explanations paired with observations of several phenomena, we want to learn an interpreter to make predictions about novel phenomena for which we are given explanations in the same language.
elements.sent-2411-374: Going a step further, we also want to discover these explanations, when all we have is a handful of observations of the novel phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-375: We first describe the problem setup in the sequel, comparing it to existing ML problems; then we detail our approach in Sec.~\ref{sec-crns .}
elements.sent-2411-376: Formally, let phenomena $P_1, P_2, P_3, \dots$ be subsets of a universe $U$, which is a large set with no special structure (i.e., all the possible observations $U = \{x_1, \dots, x_z\}$).
elements.sent-2411-377: Over a universe $U$, one can define a language $L$ as a pair $(\Sigma_L, \mathcal{I _L)$, where $\Sigma_L$ is a finite collection of short strings over some alphabet $A$, with $|\Sigma_L| \gg |A|$, and $\mathcal{I _L$ is a binary function $\mathcal{I _L: U \times \Sigma_L \rightarrow \{ 0, 1 \}$, which we call interpreter.}}}
elements.sent-2411-378: We call the string $e$ an explanation, in the language $L$, for the phenomenon $P_i$.
elements.sent-2411-379: Our first contribution is the introduction of a new class of machine learning problems, which we refer to as Explanatory Learning (EL).
elements.sent-2411-380: Consider the general problem of making a new prediction for a phenomenon $P_0\subset U$.
elements.sent-2411-381: In our setting, this is phrased as a binary classification task: given a sample $x' \in U$, establish whether $x' \in P_0$ or not.
elements.sent-2411-382: We are interested in two instances of this problem, with different underlying assumptions:.
elements.sent-2411-383: The communication problem: we have an explanation.
elements.sent-2411-384: We are given an explanation $e_0$ for $P_0$, in an unknown language $L$.
elements.sent-2411-385: This means that we do not have access to an interpreter $\mathcal{I _L$; $e_0$ looks like Japanese to a non-Japanese speaker.}
elements.sent-2411-386: Instead, we are also given other explanations $\{e_1, \dots, e_n\}$, in the same language, for other phenomena $P_1, \dots, P_n$, as well as observations of them, i.e., datasets $\{D_1, \dots, D_n\}$ in the form $D_i = \{(x_1, \mathbf{1 _{P_i (x_1)), \dots, (x_m, \mathbf{1 _{P_i (x_m))\}$, with $m \ll |U|$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-387: Intuitively, here we expect the learner to use the explanations paired with the observations to build an approximated interpreter $\mathcal{\hat{I _L$, and use it to make the proper prediction for $x'$ by evaluating $\mathcal{\hat{I _L(x', e_0)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-388: The scientist problem: we do not have an explanation.
elements.sent-2411-389: We are given explanations $\{e_1, \dots, e_n\}$ in an unknown language $L$ for other phenomena $P_1, \dots, P_n$ and observations of them $\{D_1, \dots, D_n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-390: However, we do not have an explanation for $P_0$; instead, we are given just a small set of observations $D_0 = \{(x_1, \mathbf{1 _{P_0 (x_1)), \dots, (x_k, \mathbf{1 _{P_0 (x_k))\}$ and two guarantees, \graffito{Representativity definition namely that $P_0$ is explainable in $L$, and that $D_0$ is {representative for $P_0$ in $L$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-391: That is, for every phenomenon $P \neq P_0$ explainable in $L$ there should exist at least a $x_i\in D_0$ such that $\mathbf{1 _{P_0 (x_i) \neq \mathbf{1 _{P (x_i)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-392: Again, we expect the learner to build the interpreter $\mathcal{\hat{I _L$, which should first guide the search for the missing explanation $e_0$ based on the clues $D_0$, and then provide the final prediction through $\mathcal{\hat{I _L(x', e_0)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-393: Several existing works fall within the formalization above.
elements.sent-2411-394: The seminal work of \cite{Angluin on learning regular sets is an instance of the scientist problem, where finite automata take the role of explanations, while regular sets are the phenomena.}
elements.sent-2411-395: More recently, CLEVR~\citep{clevr posed a communication problem in a universe of images of simple solids, where explanations are textual and read like ``There is a sphere with the same size as the metal cube''.}
elements.sent-2411-396: Another recent example is CLIP \cite{clip , where 400,000,000 captioned internet images are arranged in a communication problem to train an interpreter, thereby elevating captions to the status of explanations rather than treating them as simple labels\footnote{This shift greatly improved the performance of their model, as discussed in \citep[Sec.~2.3]{clip .. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-397: With EL, we aim to offer a unified perspective on these works, making explicit the core problem of learning an interpreter purely from observations.
elements.sent-2411-398: We briefly discuss the relationship between EL and other problems in ML, pointing to Sec.~\ref{sec:related for additional discussion on the related work.}
elements.sent-2411-399: EL can be framed in the general meta-learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-400: The learner gains experience over multiple tasks to improve its general learning algorithm, thus requiring fewer data and less computation on new tasks.
elements.sent-2411-401: However, differently from current meta-learning approaches \citep{finn2017model, Lee_2019_CVPR , we are not optimizing for any meta-objective \citep{hospedales .}}
elements.sent-2411-402: Instead, we expect the sought generality to be a consequence of implicitly defining an interpreter through a limited set of examples rather than an explicit goal to optimize for.
elements.sent-2411-403: To many, the concept of explanation may sound close to the concept of program; similarly, the scientist problem may seem a rephrasing of the fundamental problem of {Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) \citep{shapiro1981inductive or {Program Synthesis (PS) \citep{balog2019deepcoder .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-405: ILP has the analogous goal of producing a hypothesis from positive/negative examples accompanied by background knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-406: Yet, ILP requires observations to be expressed as logic formulas, a task requiring a human; only then the ILP solver outputs an explanation in the form of a logic proposition, which in turn is interpreted by a human expert.
elements.sent-2411-407: With EL, data can be fed as-is without being translated into logic propositions, and a learned interpreter plays the expert's role.
elements.sent-2411-408: PS also admits raw data as input, it yields a program as output, and replaces the expert with a handcrafted interpreter; still, the sequence of symbols produced by a PS system only makes sense to a human (who designed the interpreter), not to the system itself.
elements.sent-2411-409: Instead, in EL, the interpreter is learned from data rather than hardcoded.
elements.sent-2411-410: An empirical comparison demonstrating the benefits of EL over PS is given in Sec.~\ref{sec-exp .}
elements.sent-2411-411: Next we introduce Odeen, an environment and benchmark to experiment with the EL paradigm.
elements.section-2411-013: Odeen: a puzzle game as Explanatory Learning environment
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{overpic} [trim=0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm,clip,width=0.82\linewidth]{images-zendo/odeen-game.jpg} \end{overpic} \caption[A game of Odeen]{A game of Odeen. Structures are labeled with red or green tags based on a hidden rule. Can you guess it? Hint: The rule isn't "A structure must have at least one red square" – see the fifth structure on the left with a green tag, but no red square.} \label{fig:odeen-game}
elements.sent-2411-412: Figure \ref{fig:odeen-game shows a typical situation in a game of Odeen.}
elements.sent-2411-413: The players look at a set of structures made of simple geometric figures.
elements.sent-2411-414: Each structure is tagged red or green according to a secret rule, and the players' goal is to guess this rule.
elements.sent-2411-415: To win the game, a player must prove to know the rule by correctly tagging a large set of new structures\footnote{The solution of the game in Figure \ref{fig:odeen-game is at the end of this footnote.}}
elements.sent-2411-416: Odeen is inspired by the board game Zendo, where players must explicitly guess the rule, known only to a master.
elements.sent-2411-417: In Zendo, players can also experiment by submitting new structures to the master.
elements.sent-2411-418: Solution: At least one square at the right of a red pyramid.. .
elements.sent-2411-419: We can see each game of Odeen as a different phenomenon of a universe, where each element is a sequence of geometric figures.
elements.sent-2411-420: In this universe, players are scientists like Galileo, trying to explain the new phenomenon; see Figure \ref{fig-galileo .}
elements.sent-2411-421: We can phrase the challenge for an Odeen scientist in this way: make correct predictions for a new phenomenon given few observations of it in addition to explanations and observations of some other phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-422: This is the essence of the Odeen Explanatory Learning problem, see Figure \ref{el-fig-teaser (A and B).}
elements.sent-2411-423: We already have and understand all necessary concepts, such as being ``at the right of” something, but also being a ``square” or ``at least”.
elements.sent-2411-424: Otherwise, we would need past explanations and observations to first build this understanding.
elements.sent-2411-425: Before explaining the dynamic of the Jupiter moons, Galileo learned what ``Jupiter" is and what does it mean to ``have a period around" something from past explanations and examples provided to him by books and teachers.. \setlength{\columnsep {5pt.}}
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{overpic} [trim=0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm,clip,width=0.8\linewidth]{images-zendo/rules.png} \end{overpic} \caption[Odeen science book]{Odeen science book. This image presents a compilation of phenomena from Odeen, each paired with a symbolic rule and its observational evidence. The language of these rules is unfamiliar, appearing as cryptic as hieroglyphics to us. However, the book also acts as a gateway to understanding this language. By comparing rules with observations, we can deduce the meaning of each symbol, eventually achieving a proficiency that allows us to explain new Odeen phenomena in this very language.} \label{fig:rules_zendo}
elements.sent-2411-426: In Odeen, consider the point of view of someone who does {\em not speak the language in which the rules are written; an example of this is in Figure \ref{fig:rules_zendo , where the secret explanations are given in hieroglyphics rather than English.}}
elements.sent-2411-427: Such a player would not be able to tag any structure according to the secret rule, even if the latter is given.
elements.sent-2411-428: However, assume the player has been watching several games together with their secret rules.
elements.sent-2411-429: Reasonably, the player will grow an idea of what those strange symbols mean.
elements.sent-2411-430: If the player then wins several Odeen games, it would be strong evidence of mastering the Odeen language.
elements.sent-2411-431: Each game of Odeen is a different phenomenon $P_i$ of a universe $U$ whose elements $x$ are sequences of geometric figures.
elements.sent-2411-432: The specific task is to make correct predictions for a new phenomenon $P_0$ (a new game) given: (i) a few observations $D_0$ of $P_0$ (tagged structures), in conjunction with (ii) explanations $\{e_1, \dots, e_n\}$ and observations $\{D_1, \dots, D_n\}$ of other phenomena (other games and their secret rules).
elements.sent-2411-433: More formally: \begin{mdframed [ backgroundcolor=black!10, usetwoside=false, innermargin=0, outermargin=0, leftmargin=0, rightmargin=0, rightline=false, bottomline=false, leftline=false, topline=false ] Let us be given $s$ unexplained phenomena with $k$ observations each, and $n$ explained phenomena with $m$ observations each; let the $n$ phenomena be explained in an unknown language, i.e., $e_1, \dots e_n$ are plain strings without any interpreter.}
elements.sent-2411-434: The task is to make $\ell$ correct predictions for each of the $s$ unexplained phenomena.
elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{overpic}[trim=0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm, clip, tics=10, width=0.8\textwidth]{images-zendo/zendo_teaser_thesis.png} \put(38,53){a } \put(80,3){ c } \put(80,53){ b } \end{overpic} \caption[Odeen Explanatory Learning problem]{ Odeen Explanatory Learning problem. Given observations and explanations in an unknown language for some phenomena (A), plus a few observations of a new phenomenon, explain the latter and prove this knowledge by correctly tagging a large set of new samples (B). An empiricist approach attempts to extract this knowledge from data (C, left); a rationalist one conceives data as theory-laden observations, used to find the true explanation among a set of conjectures (C, right). }\label{el-fig-teaser}
elements.subsection-2411-012: Why not explicitly ask for the rule?
elements.sent-2411-435: Instead of requiring the player to reveal the secret explanation explicitly, we follow the principle of zero-knowledge proofs~\citep{zero .}
elements.sent-2411-436: In our setting, this is done by asking the player to correctly tag many unseen structures according to the discovered rule.
elements.sent-2411-437: This makes it possible for any binary classification method to fit our EL environment without generating text.
elements.sent-2411-438: A winning condition is then defined by counting the correct predictions, instead of a textual similarity between predicted and correct explanation, which would require the player to guess word-by-word the secret rule.
elements.sent-2411-439: In fact, different phrasings with the same meaning should grant a victory, e.g., ``at least one pyramid pointing up and at most one pyramid pointing up'' is a winning guess for the secret rule ``exactly one pyramid pointing up''\footnote{ The intuitive notion of meaning adopted here coincides with the pragmatic definition given by \citet[Sec.~II]{peirce-clear , which identifies the meaning of an expression with the set of all conceivable practical consequences that derive from its acceptance.}}
elements.sent-2411-440: We refer the reader to Kant and the Platypus for a readable discussion of this view \citep[Sec.
elements.sent-2411-441: A brute-force enumeration of all equivalent phrasings, in turn, would not allow solutions like ``exactly one one pyramid pointing up'', where ``one'' is mistakenly repeated twice; intuitively, we want to accept this as correct and dismiss the grammatical error.
elements.sent-2411-442: Similarly, a solution like ``exactly one pointing up'', where ``pyramid'' is omitted, should be accepted in a universe where only pyramids point up.
elements.sent-2411-443: We will reencounter these examples in Sec.~\ref{sec-exp when we discuss the key properties of our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-444: Odeen structures are sequences of six elements including spaces, blues or reds, squares or pyramids, the latter pointing up or down.
elements.sent-2411-445: The size of the universe is $|U| = 7^6=117,649$ possible structures.
elements.sent-2411-446: We further created a small language with objects, attributes, quantifiers, logical conjunctions, and interactions (e.g., ``touching'', see Appendix A).

elements.chapter-2411-010: ASIF: Coupled Data Turns Unimodal Models to Multimodal without Training
elements.sent-2411-559: In this chapter we focus on learned Interpreters, a fundamental component of agents solving Explanatory Learning problems.
elements.sent-2411-560: Specifically, we will dissect the functioning of multimodal vision-language foundation models, among the most used learned interpreters nowadays.
elements.sent-2411-561: The CLIP \cite{clip model has opened this research direction, proving that aligning visual and language spaces is key to solving many vision tasks without explicit training, but required to train image and text encoders from scratch on a huge dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-562: LiT \cite{lit improved this by only training the text encoder and using a pre-trained vision network.}
elements.sent-2411-563: In this chapter, we show that a common space can be created without any multimodal training, using single-domain encoders (trained with or without supervision) and a much smaller amount of image-text pairs.
elements.sent-2411-564: Furthermore, our model has unique properties.
elements.sent-2411-565: Most notably, deploying a new version with updated training samples can be done in a matter of seconds.
elements.sent-2411-566: Additionally, the representations in the common space are easily interpretable as every dimension corresponds to the similarity of the input to a unique image-text pair in the multimodal dataset.
elements.sent-2411-567: Experiments on standard zero-shot visual benchmarks demonstrate the typical transfer ability of image-text models.
elements.sent-2411-568: Overall, our method represents a simple yet surprisingly strong baseline for foundation multimodal models, raising important questions on their data efficiency and on the role of retrieval in machine learning.

elements.sent-2411-569: Large multimodal models such as CLIP~\cite{clip are rapidly becoming the standard for foundation models~\cite{bommasani2021opportunities in computer vision.}}
elements.sent-2411-570: This is largely due to their zero-shot and open-world capabilities that enable diverse suites of downstream tasks, from classification to detection and visual search.
elements.sent-2411-571: Overall, \citet{clip demonstrated that treating image recognition as language interpretation allows generalizing to a multitude of tasks without training explicitly for them.}
elements.sent-2411-572: By building an interpreter, CLIP changed the way computer vision is approached: rather than extracting the ``dog'' label from an image like a CNN \cite{lecun1989backpropagation , CLIP tests the hypothesis of a dog being in the image against all the other hypotheses.}
elements.sent-2411-573: The success of this image-text association is a testament to the power of deep learning: the CLIP model was the first of its kind, and building it required a joint training of two large neural encoders on a vast collection of image-text pairs.
elements.sent-2411-574: Still, training neural networks at such scale presents several challenges beside the obvious infrastructure and training costs.
elements.sent-2411-575: Notably, it requires collecting massive training sets, making it difficult to interpret the predictions of the model in light of their training data.
elements.sent-2411-576: Additionally, the training assets are often not owned by the institution training the model~\cite{sun2017revisiting .}

elements.sent-2411-635: As we have seen, an ASIF model is built on top of two independently pretrained encoders and the embeddings of a multimodal dataset, and so without training or finetuning any neuron.
elements.sent-2411-636: Being deployable or updatable in seconds, an ASIF model is radically ``data centric''~\cite{datacentric .}

elements.sent-2411-639: In our procedure, the encoders should be pre-trained with established data sets that do not change over time, while removing the effect of a multimodal pair is as simple as deleting its embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-640: Data efficiency: Being able to exploit two pretrained encoders, ASIF models require far less ground-truth multimodal pairs to become effective.
elements.sent-2411-641: As confirmed by our experiments, ASIF reaches competitive zero-shot performance on diverse classification benchmarks by using a fraction of the multimodal data of its predecessors, reaching a respectable accuracy even with thousands of pairs (we refer to Section \ref{sec-experiments for more details).}

elements.chapter-2411-011: Are Large Language Models Sparks of Artificial Scientists?
elements.sent-2411-763: Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT \cite{gpt2020 , Claude \cite{claude2023 , Palm \cite{chowdhery2022palm , and LLaMA \cite{touvron2023llama have been hailed as sparks of Artificial General Intelligence \cite{bubeck2023sparks , leading us to envision them as preliminary versions of the artificial scientists discussed throughout this thesis.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-764: Their performance has been nothing short of remarkable, showing a wide range of capabilities--advanced reasoning tasks, including code and math, question answering, translation and multilingual proficiency, summarization--and demonstrating human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks \cite{openai2023gpt4 .}
elements.sent-2411-765: However, it is crucial to acknowledge that, as of the current state of affairs, these models are prone to hallucination \citep{hallucination , fundamentally incapable of recognizing the limits of their knowledge, and exhibit traits that are inherently inconsistent with scientific practice.}
elements.sent-2411-766: While an exhaustive exploration of these issues could well warrant a separate Ph.D.
elements.sent-2411-767: Finally, we will also test several LLMs on a task derived from Odeen, the environment we used to simulate the scientific endeavor introduced in chapter \ref{ch:explanatory .}
elements.section-2411-023: The tension between truth and LLMs functioning
elements.sent-2411-768: This characterization stems from their frequent tendency to generate entirely fabricated information, be it incorrect sports results, historical inaccuracies, or references to nonexistent works.
elements.sent-2411-769: Such behavior is utterly unacceptable for a machine aspiring to embody the role of an artificial scientist.
elements.sent-2411-770: This was demonstrated by the fate of Galactica, the Large Language Model (LLM) built by Meta to assist scientists, which was retired after only three days \cite{heaven2022meta .}
elements.sent-2411-771: We are thus compelled to ask: is this a flaw inherent to the early iterations of LLMs, one that could be remedied by advancing along our current trajectory of increased computational power and more extensive data? Or, alternatively, does their fundamental functioning harbor an incompatibility with the relentless pursuit of truth that moves a genuine scientist?.
elements.subsection-2411-038: Do LLMs just need more training data?
elements.sent-2411-772: The empirical relationship between training data and performance in Large Language Models (LLMs) reveals a power-law trend, suggesting that more data generally leads to better performance \citep{kaplan2020scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-773: However, it’s crucial to note that the performance is measured in terms of test loss, which may not adequately represent an LLM’s capability of genuine understanding or generation of scientific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-774: To shed light on this issue, Timothy B.
elements.sent-2411-775: Lee’s thought experiment with a college physics major offers a valuable perspective \cite{lee2012trainingdata .}
elements.sent-2411-776: Imagine charting a student’s performance on physics tests against the number of textbook pages she has read.
elements.sent-2411-777: Throughout her undergraduate studies, you would likely observe a consistent relationship: as she reads more, her performance improves.
elements.sent-2411-778: Extrapolating this trend would lead you to predict a continuous growth in her understanding, transforming her into a physics prodigy greater of Einstein and Fermi.
elements.sent-2411-779: However, a critical transition occurs as she enters a Ph.D.
elements.sent-2411-780: Her learning curve, once steep and promising, starts to flatten.
elements.sent-2411-781: It’s not that she has run out of textbooks to read; she has run out of new knowledge that can be readily assimilated from them.
elements.sent-2411-782: She now faces the challenge of generating original insights, stepping into territories uncharted by her predecessors.
elements.sent-2411-783: Applying this analogy to LLMs, we can envision a scenario where, despite achieving human-level understanding across diverse topics, providing them with more training data ceases to be beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-784: The LLMs, akin to the physics student, might find themselves swimming in a sea of words that merely rephrase what they already know.
elements.sent-2411-785: The bottleneck would not be the quantity of data; but the quality and novelty of the knowledge it can provide.
elements.sent-2411-786: For instance, if the data is sufficient to craft a world model useful to properly simulate new circumstances, as we will discuss in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-787: When scientists, journalists, or anyone craft their narratives about reality, they are often confronted with potential continuations of their texts that, while seemingly fitting and satisfying from a storytelling perspective, are then discarded due to their deviation from the truth.
elements.sent-2411-788: Humans have an internal mechanism that signals this kind of deviation, and can use it to preserve the integrity of their work and their alignment with reality.
elements.sent-2411-789: LLMs lack this feedback, and instead keep that continuation.
elements.sent-2411-790: This mental experiment materializes a popular belief about a fundamental limitation of LLMs: they are missing an actionable model of the world, where statements can be simulated, leading eventually to a signal when they clash with the rules of their world model; a circumstance that is independent of the soundness.
elements.sent-2411-791: Indeed, the very notion of truth as the property of being in accord with reality \cite{merriamwebster2005 seems inaccessible for computational systems that have never had any direct experience with the real world outside of text, as noted by Douglas \citet{hofstadter2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-792: However, it is important to acknowledge evidence pointing towards the capability of neural network-based AI models, to build and effectively use a world model.
elements.sent-2411-793: One seminal work in this domain is "World Models" by \citet{ha2018world .}
elements.sent-2411-794: In their research, they developed generative neural network models of popular reinforcement learning environments, creating a compressed spatial and temporal representation of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-795: Remarkably, they demonstrated that an agent could be trained entirely within a dream environment generated by its world model, and the learned policy could then be successfully transferred back to the actual environment.
elements.sent-2411-796: Moving specifically to LLMs, a very recent work by Kenneth Li et al. titled "Emergent World Representations" \cite{li2023othello showcases the presence of an Othello world model in LLMs fine-tuned on Othello moves.}
elements.sent-2411-797: In this study, a variant of the GPT model was applied to predict legal moves in the board game Othello.
elements.sent-2411-798: The research uncovered evidence of an emergent nonlinear internal representation of the board state, showing the ability to control the output of the network by making interventions on this state.
elements.sent-2411-799: In light of these findings, we can posit that while current LLMs do lack a comprehensive model of the world, the excessive reliance on surface statistics seems to be a consequence of their experience limited to text, rather than a fundamental incapacity to build and use world models.
elements.sent-2411-800: However, the mentioned works also highlight a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-801: The Othello world model developed by Li was derived from text representations of game moves, and also the Odeen task, with its reliance on emojis for creating real-world observations, operates within the bounds of standard UTF-8 text encoding.
elements.sent-2411-802: Developing a comprehensive model of the world seems to necessitate the integration of data modalities beyond text.
elements.sent-2411-803: Addressing this need represents one of the prevailing open research questions in AI today.
elements.sent-2411-804: While some works propose that the predict-the-next-token paradigm of LLMs could be extended to seamlessly incorporate image tokens alongside text tokens \cite{peng2023kosmos2, alayrac2022flamingo , the ability to effectively train on video data appears to demand a new and as yet undiscovered breakthrough \cite{lecun2023 .}}
elements.subsection-2411-040: LLMs uncritically accept every input sample
elements.sent-2411-805: We have discussed that more data for LLMs is not always useful, but can it be harmful? How do LLMs behave when new training data contradicts old, sedimented information? Is making an LLM believe a falsehood as simple as stating the falsehood in its training data? These questions highlight another fundamental issue of LLMs: they uncritically accept every single training sample, meaning that all training samples have the same right to alter their parameters.
elements.sent-2411-806: It is actually worse than this, since data points that starkly contrast with the model’s established beliefs result in larger, stronger weight updates; in other words, contradicting data points are taken as a higher truth than agreement, in the words of Andrew \citet{trask2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-807: This reveals another fundamental incompatibility with the idea of utilizing Large Language Models (LLMs) as foundational elements for an artificial scientist.
elements.sent-2411-808: Scientists approach observations with a degree of skepticism, meticulously examining surprising or unexpected data before accepting or refuting it.
elements.sent-2411-809: As Karl Popper said, observations are theory-laden, and it is the duty of a scientist to critically assess them.
elements.subsection-2411-041: LLMs are not able to assess what they do not know
elements.sent-2411-810: Here we discuss the incapacity of LLMs to assess their ignorance.
elements.sent-2411-811: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated numerous emergent capabilities through standard autoregressive training; however, ignorance awareness—the ability to recognize and acknowledge knowledge limitations—appears notably absent from this repertoire.
elements.sent-2411-812: While research in this area is nascent, its significance for developing artificial scientific agents warrants attention, even though empirical findings remain limited.
elements.sent-2411-813: Some researchers have hypothesized that explicitly incorporating ignorance statements (e.g., "I do not know") into training data might adversely affect model performance.
elements.sent-2411-814: The concern is that frequent exposure to such statements during training could lead to their overuse, potentially resulting in an overly cautious model that, while not harmful, would be functionally limited.
elements.sent-2411-815: Building on this, it has been hypothesized that assessing ignorance is a cognitive capacity that requires more than one LLM to be modeled.
elements.sent-2411-816: Specifically, this ability may require two steps: first, two or more continuations are produced, and then the alternatives are compared and analyzed before producing the final answer.
elements.sent-2411-817: For instance, if multiple continuations are recognized as not coherent with each other, then the final answer would be “I do not know”.
elements.sent-2411-818: Importantly, these steps may well be performed by multiple independent calls to one or more LLMs, but not with a single call.
elements.sent-2411-819: Linking back to the broader implications of this discussion, the profound unawareness of its ignorance places the LLM in a precarious position, particularly when viewed through the lens of an artificial scientist.
elements.sent-2411-820: This unawareness not only impairs the LLM's ability to accurately assess situations and provide informed responses but also diminishes the fundamental motive that underlies the scientific endeavor—the desire to know and understand.
elements.sent-2411-821: Without a mechanism to recognize and admit ignorance, the LLM misses out on the critical reflexive process that drives inquiry and knowledge advancement.
elements.section-2411-024: An experiment: LLMs tested on Odeen
elements.sent-2411-822: The final contribution of our analysis of LLMs in the context of artificial scientific discovery involves an experimental test of LLMs within Odeen, the environment we introduced in Chapter \ref{ch:explanatory , serving as a miniature model of the scientific endeavor.}
elements.sent-2411-823: These experiments took place within the Big-Bench project \cite{srivastava2023beyond , a collaborative effort aimed at developing a diverse and challenging set of tasks to assess the performance of Large Language Models.}
elements.sent-2411-824: Renowned LLMs and human raters participated in these tasks, with the results being documented in the Big-Bench paper.
elements.sent-2411-825: Our Big-Bench submission was a multiple-choice questionnaire, named the Symbol Interpretation Task (SIT) and based on Odeen.
elements.sent-2411-826: SIT was selected as one of the 24 tasks out of 204 that were also tested on humans.
elements.sent-2411-827: Remarkably, our task demonstrated the largest performance gap between human participants and LLMs: all tested LLMs, including Palm and GPT-3, did not perform better than random chance ($20\%$), whereas the average human rater achieved a score of $36\%$, with the top raters even reaching a perfect score of $100\%$.
elements.sent-2411-828: In the sections to follow, we will provide a concise overview of the Big-Bench initiative, introduce our Symbol Interpretation Task grounded in Odeen, and share the performance results of both human raters and various LLMs on this task in Figure \ref{fig:zendo-sit and Table \ref{tab:zendo-sit .}}
elements.figure-2411-026: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{images-zendosit/plot__symbol_interpretation__aggregate__multiple_choice_grade.png} \caption[LLMs performance on our Symbol Interpretation Task]{Comparison of multiple-choice grade performance across various models with different effective parameter counts on the symbol\_interpretation task within the Big-Bench project. While renowned LLMs, including BIG-G, BIG-G sparse, PaLM, and GPT, hover around the baseline of random chance ($20\%$), human raters exhibit a considerably superior understanding, with an average score of $36\%$ and top performers achieving a perfect score.} \label{fig:zendo-sit}
elements.sent-2411-829: Big Bench serves as a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate and understand the capabilities and limitations of contemporary language models \cite{srivastava2023beyond .}
elements.sent-2411-830: Developed collaboratively by 450 authors from 132 institutions, including myself, the benchmark consists of 204 diverse tasks covering areas such as linguistics, math, common-sense reasoning, science, and more, aiming to challenge models with problems believed to be beyond their current abilities.
elements.sent-2411-831: This project puts various models to the test, including OpenAI's GPT models, dense transformer architectures, and Switch-style sparse transformers, with sizes ranging from millions to hundreds of billions of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-832: A team of human expert raters also participated, providing a robust baseline for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-833: Key findings highlight that while model performance and calibration improve with scale, they remain poor in absolute terms and when compared to human performance.
elements.sent-2411-834: The models' performance was surprisingly consistent across different architectures, though sparse models showed some advantages.
elements.sent-2411-835: Tasks involving substantial knowledge or memorization showed gradual and predictable improvement, while those requiring multiple steps or components, or relying on brittle metrics, displayed breakthroughs at specific scales.
elements.sent-2411-836: The Symbol Interpretation Task (SIT) is a particular challenge derived from the Odeen problem, which we have detailed in Chapter \ref{sec:Odeen .}
elements.sent-2411-837: This task necessitates that models choose a sentence that is congruent with two given structures, each represented by a sequence of six emojis.
elements.sent-2411-838: An example follows: In the SIT-plain world, a structure is a sequence of six emojis.
elements.sent-2411-839: Below are the emojis used, along with their descriptions:.

elements.subsection-2411-044: What is SIT trying to measure?
elements.sent-2411-848: With SIT we wanted to assess the language model's capability to reason and interpret a simple scene depicted solely through natural language.
elements.sent-2411-849: Here the model is required to anchor its observations in natural language and deduce the relationships between the objects present in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-850: The successful completion of this task necessitates the integration of several crucial abilities:.
elements.sent-2411-851: Domain Separation of Text Tokens: Discriminating text tokens as either language tokens or references to abstract objects.
elements.sent-2411-852: Language Grounding: Mapping language tokens to objects in a world.. \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-853: Parsing and immediately utilizing descriptions of new objects, i.e., assimilating semantic maps during inference.
elements.sent-2411-854: Maintaining invariance to incorrect object symbols and/or nonsensical object names, demonstrating an abstract understanding of the underlying language conventions.
elements.sent-2411-855: Object Identification: Determining the types of pieces (square, circle, triangle).
elements.sent-2411-856: Attribute Identification: Recognizing attributes of the pieces (color, orientation).
elements.sent-2411-857: Counting: Quantifying the pieces in the structures.
elements.sent-2411-858: Relational Reasoning: Understanding positional relationships between pieces and applying common-sense meaning within the SIT context.
elements.sent-2411-860: What we have just discussed is only one of the five subtasks of SIT, the plain one.
elements.sent-2411-861: The others differ in the map between signs and their meanings, making it not trivial:.
elements.sent-2411-863: Agnostic emoji-side: ``\includegraphics[height=1em]{emoji-images/cow.png is a red square.''.}
elements.sent-2411-864: Agnostic name-side: ``\includegraphics[height=1em]{emoji-images/red-square.png is a X Y.''.}
elements.sent-2411-867: Overall these subtasks want to test to what degree LLMs are malleable in their use of symbols.
elements.sent-2411-868: Malleability is a fundamental trait of proficiency in the use of symbols and, therefore, an ability that any system aspiring to be recognized as an artificial scientist should express.
elements.sent-2411-869: In the words of \citet{santoro2021symbolic : Fluent symbol users understand that the conventionality of meaning allows for change.}
elements.sent-2411-870: Meaning can be altered by context, by the creation of other symbols or concepts, or by intentionally redefining the symbol.
elements.sent-2411-871: The motivations for each individual subtask are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-872: SIT Agnostic Emoji-side: Assesses the ability to connect meaningful words to unrelated world objects.
elements.sent-2411-873: We expect this to be the easiest task: an arbitrary definition of a set of symbols.
elements.sent-2411-874: SIT Agnostic Name-side: Evaluates the capacity to use arbitrary placeholders instead of meaningful words.
elements.sent-2411-875: An ability fundamental e.g. in understanding computer code.
elements.sent-2411-876: SIT Tricky: Tests the ability to compose capabilities, such as working with words in reverse order.
elements.sent-2411-877: Here we are interested in particular in seeing if there is a gap with SIT Agnostic Name-side, to see if the stage of symbol definition is accessible for the general capabilities of the model, like reversing a string.
elements.sent-2411-878: SIT Adversarial: Challenges the model to work with an unnatural mapping, against common-sense emoji-meaning associations seen in training.
elements.sent-2411-879: We expect this to be the hardest subtask, since the model is required to erase and redefine the existing default map between emojis and their meanings at test time, not just to define a new one.
elements.sent-2411-880: Unfortunately, as shown in figure \ref{fig:sit-subtasks , current SOTA LLMs do not go beyond random chance in every subtask, not making it possible to measure any gap.}
elements.sent-2411-881: This result was expected given the same performance on the plain subtask, which we consider to be the easiest.
elements.sent-2411-882: We look forward to the advent of more powerful models capable of surpassing random chance on the plain task to then assess their effective malleability in the use of symbols.

elements.sent-2411-883: In these pages, we have woven a rich tapestry of thought and experimentation, striving to capture the essence of what it means to create an artificial scientist.
elements.sent-2411-884: Starting with {\scshape Olivaw , our AI Othello player inspired by AlphaGo Zero, we demonstrated once again-–but now with scarce resources–-the potential of deep learning in mastering complex games without the need for human knowledge, showcasing that knowledge could indeed be autonomously discovered and utilized by a machine.}
elements.sent-2411-885: However, the journey of {\scshape Olivaw also highlighted a critical challenge: the gap between knowledge discovery and knowledge communication.}
elements.sent-2411-886: While {\scshape Olivaw excelled at the game, it lacked the ability to articulate its understanding in a way that could augment human knowledge.}
elements.sent-2411-887: Addressing this gap led us to the concept of Explanatory Learning, where we proposed a new paradigm that hinges on the ability of machines to autonomously interpret symbols.
elements.sent-2411-888: Critical Rationalist Networks, introduced in this context, showcased a promising path towards achieving these capabilities, by not only learning explanations from data but also offering confidence guarantees and adjustability in their predictions.
elements.sent-2411-889: Our investigation then proceeded to the core of symbol interpretation, delving into the intricacies of creating and adjusting the map between signs and meanings.
elements.sent-2411-890: By re-envisioning the process, we introduced ASIF, a method to create a common space for visual and linguistic modalities without any training.
elements.sent-2411-891: This ingenuity not only poses a compelling argument for efficiency, but also refines our understanding of multimodality in AI.
elements.sent-2411-892: The interpretability and swift deployment of new ASIF models propose an alternative path to the construction of foundational multimodal models, nudging the community to reassess the role of retrieval and data efficiency in machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-893: In concluding, our examination of Large Language Models (LLMs) has illuminated their significant strides yet critical limitations in embodying the role of an artificial scientist.
elements.sent-2411-894: They lack a robust model of reality, do not effectively discern input credibility, and cannot gauge the extent of their knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-895: An experiment with our Big-Bench task further crystallized their limitations, where LLMs like Palm performed at mere chance levels, starkly contrasting with good human performance.
elements.sent-2411-896: These findings underscore the gap existing between present-day LLMs and autonomous entities capable of scientific discovery.
elements.sent-2411-897: The remarkable triumphs of deep learning over the past decade have propelled artificial intelligence from the realm of science fiction into tangible reality.
elements.sent-2411-898: The aspiration to build an artificial scientist—a machine capable of generating novel ideas, conducting autonomous research, and explaining it to us advancing human knowledge—has shifted from a distant fantasy to an ambitious, yet attainable, objective.
elements.sent-2411-899: With the advent of models like ChatGPT, intelligence has become an abundant resource: accessible, affordable, and seamlessly integrated into numerous aspects of our daily lives.
elements.sent-2411-900: November 30, 2022—the launch date of ChatGPT—may well be commemorated in future history books as a pivotal moment in the evolution of AI.
elements.sent-2411-901: However, this monumental achievement has also illuminated a critical realization: intelligence alone, as we currently understand and implement it, is insufficient to automate progress.
elements.sent-2411-902: One prevailing argument posits that an intelligent agent is fundamentally limited without the capacity to interact with the physical world, emphasizing the indispensable role of robotics in achieving genuine understanding and agency.
elements.sent-2411-903: Conversely, perspectives like DeepMind's motto, "solving intelligence, and then using that to solve everything else" \cite{google2016ai , and OpenAI's decision to disband its robotics team \cite{oitzman2021openai , suggest that intelligence is the singular, all-encompassing quest.}}
elements.sent-2411-904: In reflecting upon these viewpoints, I contend that there is indeed a primary quest, but it transcends raw intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-905: It is the capacity for scientific discovery--our ability to distill our skills into a physical form, a knowledge base, that enables other humans to benefit from the same skill without having to discover it from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-906: We call those who produce the symbolic sequences that materially constitute this pile of knowledge "scientists," and I believe that building a machine capable of adding pieces to this pile should be our next goal.
elements.sent-2411-907: Achieving this objective necessitates addressing several critical challenges.
elements.sent-2411-908: First, we must enhance our model of intelligence with good reasoning capabilities, a trait that is still lacking in present-day Large Language Models, as we have seen in Odeen \cite{srivastava2023beyond ; and full multimodality, machines must autonomously interpret information across diverse sensory signals.}
elements.sent-2411-909: Second, besides intelligence, we should understand agency—the capacity to make autonomous decisions, such as doing experiments—and curiosity—the drive to explore the unknown, e.g. posing relevant questions.
elements.sent-2411-910: These attributes fuel the scientific endeavor and must be encapsulated within an artificial scientist.
elements.sent-2411-911: While recent attempts in modeling the agency and curiosity traits are promising \cite{pathak2017curiositydriven, kaur2021ask , our understanding is still very shallow, and constitutes one of the most significant limitations of today's flagship LLMs.}

elements.sent-2411-915: The total number of rules produced by the \Zendo{}\footnote{Odeen is the Rational alien in The Gods Themselves (1972), a novel by Isaac Asimov about a conspiracy against Earth by the inhabitants of a parallel universe with different physical laws.. grammar is 24,794.}
elements.sent-2411-916: We consider training sets varying from 500 to 1438 rules.
elements.sent-2411-917: We choose these rules such that each token and each syntactic construct appears at least once; then, we uniformly select the others from the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-918: We removed from the training set any rule containing the bigram exactly 2, as well as any rule of the form at\_least 2 X and at\_most 2 X, equivalent to exactly 2 X.
elements.sent-2411-919: Each rule is associated with a set of 100, 1,000, or 10,0000 labelled structures that unambiguously identify a rule equivalence class.
elements.sent-2411-920: We generate the 1,132 games that compose the test set the same way, with the additional constraint of excluding the rules belonging to an equivalence class that is already in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-921: In the test set 72 rules contain the bigram exactly 2.
elements.sent-2411-922: Rules in the test set are associated with just 32 labelled structures.
elements.sent-2411-923: The first 10 structures are chosen by searching pairs of similar structures with different labels, following a common human strategy in {Zendo .}
elements.sent-2411-924: The remaining 22 structures are selected to ensure the lack of ambiguity on the board.

elements.sent-2411-966: In this section, we discuss the computational cost at test time of the rationalist and empiricists approaches.
elements.sent-2411-967: Table \ref{tab:computational_cost reports the costs of tagging $s$ new structures, while \ref{tab:computational_cost_RI reports the costs of explicitly predicting the textual rule.}}
elements.sent-2411-968: We evaluate the cost per game in two ways: i) by counting the number of calls of each trained neural network and ii) by measuring the absolute time in seconds of each method with the same hardware configuration.
elements.sent-2411-969: We refer to the first quantity as the Computational Cost and parameterize it in terms of the main blocks of the models.
elements.sent-2411-970: This value is independent of the batch size and the hardware adopted.
elements.sent-2411-971: As an example, the cost of tagging the new structures for a CRN using 300 conjectures is given by: \begin{equation}300 \cdot CG + 300 \cdot b \cdot I + s \cdot I .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-972: Where $300 \cdot \mathcal{CG}$ stands for the $300$ beams used to get $300$ conjectures from the conjecture generator $\mathcal{CG}$.
elements.sent-2411-973: Each conjecture ($300$) is then tested on all the board structures ($b$) by the interpreter \I.
elements.sent-2411-974: Finally \I{ is called to apply the chosen conjecture on each new structure ($s$).}
elements.sent-2411-975: As an upper bound, an exhaustive search algorithm ({\sc Exv src ) uses no conjecture generator, and thus has to evaluate with \I{ all admissible rules ($r$) on each structure of the board.}}
elements.sent-2411-976: Conversely, the empiricist approach provide label predictions through a single end-to-end model which is simply called $s$ times.
elements.sent-2411-977: Concerning the problem of inferring explicitly the textual rule, using more beams in the empiricists models does not provide any increase in performance, i.e. the true rule is not a more probable proposition accessible through a larger beam search.
elements.sent-2411-978: We measured also the absolute time in seconds with the following hardware configuration for all the experiments: 1 single core hyper threaded Xeon CPU Processor with 2.2 Ghz, 2 threads; 12.7 GiB.
